page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.c1.large is a compute-optimized virtual machine instance belonging to the ecs.c1 server family. It is built on the x86_64 CPU architecture and features 8 dedicated physical cores with 1 thread per core, providing a total of 8 vCPUs. The instance is configured with 16.0 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-core ratio of 2.0 GB per core. It operates on the KVM hypervisor.

This instance does not include bundled local storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met through external block storage volumes. It does not feature any graphics processing units or complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

With a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio and dedicated CPU allocation, the ecs.c1.large is designed for compute-heavy workloads that require consistent CPU performance without hyperthreading overhead. The dedicated core allocation ensures predictable execution times, making it qualitatively cost-efficient for workloads that prioritize raw processing power over memory capacity. Suitable workloads include batch processing, web serving, distributed analytics, and compute-intensive applications that do not require local storage or GPU acceleration.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.c1.large is an x86_64 compute-optimized instance within the ecs.c1 family, running on the KVM hypervisor. It features 8 dedicated physical cores with a 1.0 thread-per-core ratio, providing 8 vCPUs and 16.0 GB of system memory. This configuration results in a resource density of 2.0 GB of RAM per core. The instance does not include bundled local storage or integrated GPUs, requiring external block storage for data persistence. With its dedicated CPU allocation and lack of hyperthreading, the ecs.c1.large delivers predictable compute performance. It is designed for compute-intensive workloads such as batch processing, web serving, and distributed analytics where consistent processing power is prioritized over high memory capacity.

tagline: A dedicated eight core compute optimized virtual machine with two gigabytes of memory per core for predictable processing workloads.
